title: How to rebuild a daily life that honors both your wounds and your growth
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The morning gap

I wake up and my body knows. It always knows before my mind catches up. There's this tightness in my chest, a knot in my stomach that says something happened. Something I can't name yet. Something that lives in the tissue before it reaches language. That's the wound. That's the part of me that still believes danger is coming, even when the room is quiet and the sun is gentle through the blinds.

And then there's the other part. The part that's read the books, done the therapy, sat with the sensations. The part that knows, intellectually, that I'm safe now. That part wants to get up, make tea, stretch, meditate, do all the right things. It wants to build a life that works. It wants to grow.

Here's the thing. Those two parts don't always get along. The wounded part thinks the growth part is naive. The growth part thinks the wounded part is holding me back. And I get stuck in the middle, frozen, unsure which voice to listen to. Sound familiar?

You're not alone in this. Most of us who've done any real recovery work live in this tension. We've touched our deepest pain, and we've tasted real change. But integrating them? Building a daily life that honors both? That's a different skill entirely. It's not taught in any manual I've found.

The problem with "just move on"

I tried the growth-only approach for years. I'd wake up, force a smile, journal about gratitude, meditate on abundance, and tell myself the past was over. I'd read books about rewiring my brain and affirm my way to freedom. And for a few hours, sometimes a few days, it almost worked. But then something would trigger me. A tone of voice. A slammed door. A sudden silence on the phone. And I'd be right back in that childhood room, small and scared and flooded.

That's when I learned about Dr. Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory. Porges showed us that the nervous system has its own logic, its own memory, its own timeline. You can't talk your way out of a trauma response. You can't think your way past a dorsal vagal collapse. The body doesn't care about your affirmations. It cares about safety. Real, felt, embodied safety. A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored, which walks through exactly how to build that kind of bottom-up safety. I wish I'd had it years ago.

So I stopped trying to bypass the wound. I stopped treating my trauma like an obstacle to be overcome and started treating it like a tenant in my own body. A tenant who needed to be heard, not evicted. That shift changed everything.

Building the container, not the content

Here's what I've come to understand. You can't plan your way out of a trauma history. You can't schedule your healing. But you can build a structure that makes space for both the wound and the growth to exist. Think of it like a garden. You don't force the plants to grow faster. You prepare the soil. You water. You pull weeds. You wait. The growth happens in its own time, but the container is your job.

For me, that container looks like three things. First, a morning check-in that's honest. I sit for five minutes and ask: What's here right now? Not what should be here. What is here? Sometimes it's grief. Sometimes it's rage. Sometimes it's a dull numbness that feels like nothing at all. I don't try to change it. I just notice. That act of noticing, without judgment, is the beginning of honoring the wound.

Second, I have a small list of non-negotiable self-care actions. They're not ambitious. They're not Instagram-worthy. They're things like: drink water, eat something with protein, step outside for three minutes, send one kind text to myself. That's it. These actions don't fix anything. But they signal to my nervous system that I'm being taken care of. Dr. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how small, rhythmic movements can help the nervous system renegotiate trauma. These tiny actions are my version of that.

Third, I leave room for the growth to show up naturally. I don't force myself to be positive. I don't chase spiritual experiences. I just stay open. And sometimes, in the middle of a hard day, something shifts. A memory loses its charge. A breath comes easier. A thought of kindness toward myself appears, unbidden. That's the growth. It's not something I create. It's something I allow.

The parts that don't want to heal

This is where it gets uncomfortable. Because not every part of you wants to heal. I know, that sounds wrong. We're supposed to want healing, right? But Dr. Richard Schwartz, founder of Internal Family Systems, has shown us that some parts of the psyche are protectors. They keep us stuck because, on some level, they believe stuck is safer than free. A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts, which explains how to befriend these protector parts instead of fighting them.

I have a part that doesn't trust peace. It's been on high alert for so long that calm feels dangerous. When things go well, that part starts scanning for the other shoe to drop. It keeps me small, keeps me preparing for disaster, because that's what kept me alive as a child. Honoring that part doesn't mean letting it run the show. It means saying, I see you. I understand why you're doing this. Thank you for keeping me safe. But we're okay now.

And here's the hard truth: that part might never fully go away. It might always be there, whispering warnings. But you can build a life where that part has a seat at the table, not the whole table. You can let it speak without letting it decide. That's the daily practice. That's the work.

I also have parts that are deeply ashamed. Parts that believe I'm broken, unlovable, too much or not enough. Dr. Janina Fisher writes about how these parts often carry the shame that wasn't ours to begin with. It was handed down. It was projected onto us. A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents, which helped me understand why those shame parts formed in the first place. Understanding the origin doesn't make the shame disappear. But it does make it feel less like a personal failing and more like a survival strategy that's now outdated.

Honoring the wound without letting it write the whole story

So what does a daily life that honors both actually look like? It's not glamorous. It's not a constant state of enlightenment. It's messy and inconsistent and full of contradictions. Some days I wake up and the wound is loud, and I spend the whole day just surviving. I cancel plans. I eat comfort food. I scroll my phone. And I tell myself: This is honoring the wound. This is what I need right now. No guilt. No shame. Just acceptance.

Other days I wake up and the growth is leading. I feel spacious. I feel curious. I try something new. I take a risk. I reach out to someone. And I tell myself: This is honoring the growth. This is what I'm capable of. Both are real. Both are me. Neither is the whole story.

Dr. Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, talks about how trauma lives in the body and how recovery requires finding ways to be fully alive in the present. That's what this is about. Not erasing the past, but expanding the present. Not silencing the wound, but giving the growth equal airtime. A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance, which taught me how to hold both the wound and the growth with the same kind of compassion.

I don't have a perfect system. I don't have a five-step plan. What I have is a willingness to stay in the tension. To let the wound speak without letting it dictate. To let the growh happen without forcing it. To build a life that has room for both the scar and the new skin growing over it.

Stay with me here. This isn't about getting it right. It's about staying in the process. It's about showing up each morning, feeling what's there, and making the smallest possible gesture toward care. Some days that gesture is a full yoga practice. Some days it's just breathing. Both count. Both matter. Both are you honoring the full, complicated, beautiful mess of being human.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did something relax? Did something tighten? That's your system responding. That's the conversation between wound and growth happening in real time. You don't have to fix it. You don't have to resolve it. You just have to stay. Stay with both. Stay with yourself.

That's the rebuild. Not a perfect life. A real one.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • No Bad Parts - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
  • Radical Acceptance - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
